ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no egg-fried-rice-specific subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fried rice as a category; no egg-fried-rice-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for egg fried rice identified in the clinical terminology hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One small serving of egg fried rice as typically plated in a Singaporean Chinese restaurant, approximately 300 g, suitable for one person as a side or light main.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, Egg — HPB Singapore Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists 'Fried Rice, Egg' as a distinct entry representing the standard Singaporean Chinese preparation of egg fried rice. Values are used directly per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fried rice GI reported as approximately 54 (low) in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), based on white rice fried with egg and oil. This is the closest peer-reviewed analogous entry for egg fried rice. GL per serving calculated as (54 × 25.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving) / 10000 = 41.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving of egg fried rice contributes approximately 960 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the 2400 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 54) and high glycemic load per serving (~41.8)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of egg fried rice is in the low range (54), the large carbohydrate load per standard 300 g serving produces a high glycemic load (~41.8), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables or protein.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from egg (82 mg per 100 g) and moderate saturated fat (1.1 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Egg fried rice contains dietary cholesterol from eggs (~246 mg per 300 g serving) and moderate saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions and choose preparations using minimal oil and reduced soy sauce to lower sodium and fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg fried rice (蛋炒饭) is a classic Chinese and Singaporean staple dish made by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice with beaten eggs in a hot wok with vegetable oil, seasoned with soy sauce, salt, and spring onions. The high-heat wok technique imparts a characteristic smoky 'wok hei' aroma and produces grains that are lightly coated in egg with a slightly crispy exterior. The texture is fluffy yet slightly chewy, with savoury umami flavour from soy sauce and egg. In Singapore, egg fried rice is served as a standalone meal or as a side dish in Chinese zi char restaurants and hawker centres. Nutritionally, a 300 g small serving provides approximately 489 kcal, 13.5 g protein, 14.4 g fat, and 77.4 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notable at approximately 960 mg per serving, which is clinically relevant for hypertensive patients. The glycemic index is approximately 54 (low), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~41.8) due to the large carbohydrate portion, making portion control important for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The dish contains egg allergens and soy (from soy sauce) and is generally considered halal when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg fried rice?
What is the GI of fried rice?
Can diabetics eat egg fried rice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, which lists 'Fried Rice, Egg' as a named entry representing the standard Singaporean Chinese preparation. GI sourced from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), a peer-reviewed journal, using the closest analogous fried rice entry. All mandatory multilingual fields are populated.
notes: Serving size of 300 g is used as a representative small (S) portion for 1–2 pax as described in the field survey listing; actual restaurant portion may vary. Trans fat is 0.0 g, confirmed as negligible in the HPB source for this preparation. Glycemic load per serving = (54 × 25.8 × 300) / 10000 = 41.8. The GI of 54 is classified as low per the ≤55 threshold used by this scoring model. The field dataing at The Seafood Market (By House of White Bee Hoon) - The Riverwalk is noted as the source context; nutrient values reflect the generic HPB Foodo composition rather than this specific restaurant's recipe. Vitamin B6 (mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for 'Fried Rice, Egg'. Vitamin B12 (mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Folate (mcg DFE) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Vitamin D (mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Vitamin E (mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Selenium (mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database.
